title: Andreola: the quiet exit from Prosecco
summary:
Forty years after Nazzareno Andreola founded the estate that bears his name, his son has spent much of his tenure doing something that looks, on paper, like commercial self-sabotage: taking the word Prosecco off the label. James Bayley met him to talk about steep hillsides, vanishing labour and the risk of giving up the most valuable word in Italian wine.

The post Andreola: the quiet exit from Prosecco appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Forty years after Nazzareno Pola founded the estate, his son has spent much of his tenure doing something that looks, on paper, like commercial self-sabotage: taking the word Prosecco off the label. James Bayley met him to talk about steep hillsides, vanishing labour and the risk of giving up the most valuable word in Italian wine. The conversation follows on from db's recent visit to Andreola's newer project in the Belluno Dolomites, Andreola takes Prosecco expertise into the Dolomites, where Stefano Pola is applying the same instinct for territory over category to a nine-hectare vineyard well outside Valdobbiadene DOCG. The times, the knowledge and the technologies of 1984 bear little relation to those of today, but the path Nazzareno Pola set out has held. "Forty years later we are still here talking about quality," his son, Stefano, tells db. Since then the estate has been built into a benchmark within Valdobbiadene DOCG, expanding on several fronts at once even as fresh challenges keep appearing. New projects and new areas of business are already under way. That growth has been rapid, moving from a small family winery selling locally to a medium-sized company with a far larger vineyard holding, a more complex winery operation and a commercial structure that now reaches export markets. Stefano counts the management of that transition among his proudest achievements, not for its scale but for what it did not cost. "I have worked to provide the right support across all production departments, creating the conditions for quality not only to be preserved, but to improve continuously as the company evolved."

Two hundred and fifty parcels, one principle

The estate now farms around 250 parcels across roughly 110 hectares, a spread that could easily have diluted the identity of any single vineyard. He bought into the Rive of Valdobbiadene DOCG at a point when other producers were doing the opposite, trading steep and difficult sites for large, flat vineyards that were easier and cheaper to farm. "Even if it means going against the current, quality comes first."

Heroism, minus the marketing

The phrase 'heroic viticulture' has drifted into most producers' vocabulary by now, deployed wherever a hillside needs dressing up for an export market. Andreola is careful to separate the term from what it actually costs on his own slopes. Climate change sits underneath everything, but the harder problem, he says, is finding people willing to do the work at all. Vineyard labour follows nature's timetable rather than anyone's rota, and that rhythm sits badly with a younger workforce. Summer starts before dawn to dodge the heat and often resumes in the evening once it has passed, a pattern that no longer suits every employee's expectations. Wet springs compound it: three or four days locked out of the vineyard by rain, followed by a single dry weekend that cannot wait for Monday. "Nature sets the timetable, and everyone involved should be willing to step in and carry out the most urgent tasks whenever the opportunity arises." What makes the territory difficult is also what makes it distinctive. Varied soils, altitudes and exposures, worked through the versatility of Glera, give Andreola a spread of styles running from Extra Brut to Dry, each one tied unmistakably to where it was grown.

The human factor

Ask Stefano who matters most in all this and the answer arrives without hesitation: the growers and vineyard workers who farm the steepest ground. "Without them, we would not have the opportunity to benefit from the excellent raw material we need in order to transform it into our wines." What strikes him about a historic wine village is how thoroughly the whole community absorbs the rhythm of the vineyard year, whether they work in it directly or not. "From the pharmacist to the mechanic, everyone has a general understanding of how to grow a vine or, in some way, how to prune it." Come September, the village itself seems to pause for harvest. It is, he says, the most beautiful element of terroir: not the soil, but the people standing on it. He is banking on that knowledge passing naturally from one generation to the next, carried more by proximity than by any formal scheme.

Dropping the name that made them famous

No decision at Andreola has drawn more scrutiny than the removal of the word Prosecco from its labels, a process Stefano began weighing in 2007 and completed by 2018. His own explanation starts with a question: Prosecco made the estate famous, but famous for what, exactly? "Every winery has to find its own interpretation," he says, and Andreola's has been to let the name of the territory do the talking instead, tying the wine unmistakably to a place already recognised for quality. Before the current appellation system arrived in 2009, the job abroad was simple: introduce people to the product and explain what it was. Now the job is subtler and harder, spent explaining the gap between a flatland Prosecco and a hillside Valdobbiadene DOCG, between a wine built for volume and one built for quality. Not everyone in the region has made peace with that gap, many of the older producers remain firmly opposed, having built the name Prosecco's fame themselves in the Conegliano Valdobbiadene area in the first place. Giving it up reads to some as a moral defeat, handing the keys of a Ferrari to producers who arrived later and now share in a reputation they did not build. Andreola's own decision, once made, came without much hand wringing. The estate no longer wanted to be tied to a Prosecco category that had grown too broad and too simplified for what it was trying to say, quite apart from the reputation the word had picked up along the way. It was not an easy call, but the results are already visible: customers now read Andreola as a producer standing apart, rooted in the historic area, practising heroic viticulture and priced accordingly on the strength of it. "We believe we made the right decision in pursuing it." He has some sympathy for wineries too frightened to make the same move, but not much patience for avoiding the question. If thirty years of marketing has hung on the word Prosecco in large type and an affordable price, then dropping it is a real risk. But if the story has always been about territory and a handcrafted, high-quality product, the word may simply no longer be doing much work. "Then perhaps that message has already become strong enough, and the word Prosecco may no longer be necessary," Stefano concludes.

Tasting notes by Patrick Schmitt MW:

1 – 26°I° Valdobbiadene DOCG Rive di Col San Martino Extra Brut Orchard fruit aromatics, dominated by fresh pear, and a floral note of star jasmine. The palate is zesty, fresh, with creamy bubbles and a lemony finish. Ripe pear and apple fruit feature first, while the finish is quite firm – this is a pure, clean and fruity but very dry style of Prosecco. 2 – DIRUPO Valdobbiadene DOCG Brut Creamy and appealing nose with a touch of pineapple and peach, ripe and sweet smelling. The palate is rounded and full, with sweet peach and some pineapple chunk. Fine bubbles provide a cleansing sensation, augmented by some fresh, balancing acidity on the finish. An easy drinking, refreshing, slightly sweet style of first-rate Prosecco, with some bitter pith on the finish, and lots of flavour persistence. 3 – Mas de Fer Rive di Soligo Valdobbiadene DOCG Extra Dry Enticing aromas of ripe orchard fruits. The palate is rich in creamy-textured bubbles, ripe apple and pear fruit, then lovely juicy peach, and a pleasant mouth-filling sweetness. The finish is fresh, with some crisp apple and lemon zest. Lingering, with some fine phenolic grip, then a subtle bitter lemon edge. Sweetest of the three, but still refreshing.

title: Lucky Saint and Ossett Brewery join Cask Marque
summary:
Beer quality accreditation organisation Cask Marque has welcomed Lucky Saint and Ossett Brewery as corporate members. Jessica Mason reports.

The post Lucky Saint and Ossett Brewery join Cask Marque appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Beer quality accreditation organisation Cask Marque has welcomed Lucky Saint and Ossett Brewery as corporate members. Jessica Mason reports. The companies join the stakeholders in the not-for-profit scheme which currently has 41 members made up of brewers, pub retailers and trade associations. Ossett Brewery currently has all of its own pubs accredited with Cask Marque already. But, by joining as a corporate member, the new move will allow Ossett to share benefits with its free trade outlets while also receiving beer quality reports on products assessed by Cask Marque anywhere in the UK. Lucky Saint joins as Cask Marque expands accreditations to include all beer with the new Perfect Pint Promise accreditation. With the rapid growth of the low and no alcohol category, the UK’s biggest independent alcohol-free beer brand continues to ensure standards are high through its own quality programme.

Improving beer quality and yields

Describing the reasons behind becoming a corporate member, Ossett Brewery managing director Edd Simpson said: ”We have worked with Cask Marque in our own pub estate and seen the benefits. We now want to take this support to our free trade customers so they can improve yields and with improved beer quality, their rate of sale and ensure our products are served in the best possible condition.” Speaking to the drinks business, Cask Marque executive director Paul Nunny admitted that he recognised the "potential" within the burgeoning low and no beer sector and said: “Draught in low and no is effectively a new sector with great potential growth.” Making assurances about beer quality checks, Nunny told db that Cask Marque has “set standards on production through Salsa + Beer that check beer quality at the brewery gate” and also added that the accreditation organisation has “worked with BBPA and gained their input and approval”. Nunny explained that “in pubs, beer lines must be cleaned every seven days as beer with no alcohol can quickly become contaminated and can increase in alcohol strength” and revealed that, because of this the organisation is already “working closely with Diageo and Heineken and now Lucky Saint”.

A shrewd move as the category continues to grow

Highlighting the fact that "quality has always been fundamental to Lucky Saint and speaking more broadly on how delivering on that quality ad supporting pubs and people with the best experiences possible was fundamental,  Lucky Saint on-trade director Will Snowden said: "From brewing to dispense, every stage plays a role in delivering a great alcohol-free beer experience. As more venues expand their alcohol-free draught offer, maintaining consistently high standards is vital. It’s an area we think deserves a lot of time and attention, and I'm very pleased to be working closer with Cask Marque as their first-ever alcohol-free beer member. Alongside our own Superior Serve programme, the Perfect Pint Promise accreditation will help support pubs in delivering the highest quality alcohol-free beer as this category continues to grow.” Looking at the business angle too, Nunny additionally pointed out that, for a pub, “having a product on draught can increase throughputs by seven times” but, he warned that it was only suitable for some pubs with the throughput because the publican “must sell a keg a week to maintain beer quality”.

Customers' expectations have never been higher

He also pointed out how important the accreditation was and insisted that “customers’ expectations on their visit to the pub have never been higher and these need to be satisfied” and highlighted that “this can be achieved by serving great beer and having trained bar staff". Offering reassurances, Nunny said: "This is why we now star rate cellar like the food hygiene ratings and have widened our accreditations to include Cask Marque and well as the new Perfect Pint Promise accreditation covering all beer". This, he reiterated, was why it was so important to have the support of beer businesses that are also interested in maintaining the calibre of beer brands they make available to pubs and bars. Not just when the beer leaves the brewery, but at each venue, in each cellar and at each point of sale. He added: "Having corporate members support is so important to deliver our programme on the perfect serve. The input from our new members will assist us to achieve this objective.”

title: Reasons to visit ProWine Mumbai
summary:
ProWine is set to take place on November 18–19, 2026, at the Jio World Convention Centre, Mumbai. db finds out more about what to expect.

The post Reasons to visit ProWine Mumbai appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
ProWine is set to take place on November 18–19, 2026, at the Jio World Convention Centre, Mumbai. db finds out more about what to expect. ProWine Mumbai brings together an unmatched mix of Indian and international wine and spirits producers, importers, distributors, country pavilions, trade promotion bodies, and allied beverage businesses. Jointly organised by Messe Düsseldorf India and All Things Nice, ProWine Mumbai is set to strengthen its position as the definitive business platform connecting global producers with India’s rapidly evolving beverage market. Bringing together industry leaders, importers, distributors, HORECA professionals, and premium consumers, the 2025 edition hosted more than 200 exhibitors, welcomed over 6,000 attendees, and saw participation from more than 20 countries, showcasing over 1,000 labels from across the globe.

Strong international representation

This year, while the final exhibitor roster continues to evolve, attendees can expect participation from leading global wine regions alongside premium Indian producers, craft beverage companies, spirits brands, and suppliers across packaging, technology, logistics, glassware, and accessories. The event has historically featured strong international representation through country pavilions from markets including France, Italy, Australia, Japan, Ireland, Germany, the UK, the US and others.

A place to gain insight

Speaking to the drinks business about what makes the show a must-visit event, ProWine Mumbai project director Rajeev Naresh said: “For wine and spirits professionals, ProWine Mumbai is more than a trade exhibition—it's a business platform. It offers opportunities to discover new products, meet producers and importers face-to-face, identify distribution partners, understand emerging consumer trends, attend expert-led masterclasses, and gain insights into India's rapidly evolving beverage market.” Naresh explained that “the combination of high-quality networking and curated educational content makes it an essential event for anyone looking to grow their business in the sector”. Mumbai is India's commercial and financial capital and the country's most influential market for premium wines and spirits. It is home to many of India's leading importers, distributors, hospitality groups, retailers, and beverage decision-makers, making it the ideal location for meaningful business conversations.  

Conveniently located

Giving more explanation about the event’s location, Naresh told db that “its proximity to Nashik, India's largest wine-producing region, excellent international connectivity, access to the country's busiest beverage import gateway, and world-class exhibition infrastructure further strengthen its position as the natural home for ProWine Mumbai”. Additionally, Naresh explained that “what differentiates ProWine Mumbai is its ability to combine business with education and innovation. Beyond the exhibition floor, visitors can experience curated masterclasses, tastings at the booths, panel discussions, bartender competitions and the newly introduced ProWine Zero segment, reflecting growing consumer interest in premium spirits and low- and no-alcohol beverages. These features ensure attendees leave with both valuable commercial connections and a deeper understanding of emerging market trends”. Looking at what is achievable, he pointed out that “the overarching goal of ProWine Mumbai is to strengthen its position as India's definitive international business platform for the wine and spirits industry while facilitating meaningful trade opportunities between domestic and global stakeholders”.

Unmissable business potential for the drinks sector

Indeed, Naresh also noted that “success is measured not only by growth in exhibitor and visitor numbers, but also by the quality of business generated, international participation, buyer engagement, educational programming, and the long-term partnerships formed through the event.” He insisted: “One of the strongest indicators of the show's impact is the growing number of success stories that have emerged from previous editions. Several international wine and spirits producers that first showcased their portfolios at ProWine Mumbai have since secured import and distribution partnerships and successfully entered the Indian market.”  

A launchpad for brands to crack the Indian market

Naresh described how “these outcomes demonstrate the event's role as more than just a trade fair—it is a launchpad for brands seeking to establish a presence in one of the world's most dynamic and promising beverage markets.” Considering its format, Naresh reiterated that the success of each show “is also evaluated through metrics such as the number of participating countries, country pavilions, trade professionals attending, business meetings facilitated, and the expansion of emerging beverage categories represented on the show floor” Naresh reinforced that the event was not just important, but provided a “gateway” for drinks business owners to successfully establish themselves in India and added that “combined with the increasing number of brands using the platform to successfully enter India, the continued growth in exhibitor participation and international representation reinforces ProWine Mumbai's position as the preferred gateway for global wine and spirits companies looking to build long-term business in the Indian market”.

title: Why Mayfair’s BiBi Lates is worth staying out for
summary:
London restaurant BiBi has introduced a new late-night format, BiBi Lates, giving guests the chance to experience signature dishes alongside an expanded cocktail offering after dinner service.

The post Why Mayfair’s BiBi Lates is worth staying out for appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
London restaurant BiBi has introduced a new late-night format, BiBi Lates, giving guests the chance to experience signature dishes alongside an expanded cocktail offering after dinner service.

Award-winning London restaurant BiBi has launched BiBi Lates, a new evening concept designed to extend service beyond its regular dinner offering.

Available from 9pm onwards, the new format pairs an extended cocktail list with an à la carte menu featuring a selection of dishes from the restaurant's tasting menu.

Signature dishes served after dark

Guests visiting BiBi Lates can order dishes including Mangalori Scallops and Launceston Lamb Barra Kebab, offering a more flexible way to experience chef Chet Sharma's cooking without committing to the full tasting menu.

The restaurant said the new format is designed to allow diners to enjoy BiBi's atmosphere later into the evening while sampling a curated selection of its signature dishes.

BiBi takes its name from the South Asian word that formally means "lady of the house", although Sharma has adopted its colloquial Urdu meaning of "grandma" as a tribute to his two grandmothers, whose influence is woven throughout the restaurant.

Research-led approach

Opened in September 2021, BiBi's menus are underpinned by years of research into ingredients, with an emphasis on traceability and ethics. The restaurant sources dayboat-landed fish from British waters, hand-dived scallops from Devon, buffalo milk from Lancashire and cheese from Somerset, while using heritage rice varieties and single-source spices from the Indian subcontinent.

The kitchen also follows sustainability and zero-waste practices, including composting, recycling, fermentation, preservation and maximising the use of individual ingredients.

Alongside its food offering, BiBi serves a wine list designed to complement the menus, as well as reinvented classic cocktails, non-alcoholic juices and cold-brewed teas. The sommelier team, led by Wilem Powell, offers two wine pairings inspired by Sharma's grandfathers.

Since opening, the Mayfair restaurant has received a number of industry accolades, including Best Cocktail List of the Year at the National Restaurant Awards 2025.

title: Vintae completes Bodegas Riojanas acquisition
summary:
Vintae has completed its acquisition of Bodegas Riojanas after the registration of the winery's restructuring plan, giving the Rioja-based wine group a 90% stake in the historic producer.

The post Vintae completes Bodegas Riojanas acquisition appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Vintae has completed its acquisition of Bodegas Riojanas after the registration of the winery's restructuring plan, giving the Rioja-based wine group a 90% stake in the historic producer.

Vintae has completed its acquisition of Bodegas Riojanas after the registration of the winery's Restructuring Plan with the Commercial Registry, formalising the entry of its holding company, Gevisa Wine Capital, as the majority shareholder.

The registration follows approval by Commercial Court No. 6 of Logroño and completes a process that began in May. Bodegas Riojanas will retain its status as a publicly listed company, with its shares continuing to trade on the Spanish continuous market.

Under the Restructuring Plan, Gevisa Wine Capital has acquired a 90% stake in Bodegas Riojanas through a capital reduction followed by a simultaneous capital increase via the capitalisation of debt.

The transaction represents a €12 million investment by Gevisa Wine Capital, following an agreement with the financial institutions that adhered to the Restructuring Plan. Vintae will also provide Bodegas Riojanas with a financing facility of up to €5 million to support the next phase of the business.

Board changes

The registration of the Restructuring Plan also brings changes to the board of Bodegas Riojanas.

Ricardo Arambarri Pérez has been appointed chairman, while Fernando Martínez Sampedro becomes chief executive officer. Isabel García Castellanos and Raúl Acha Salazar have joined the board as proprietary directors.

The company said two independent directors will be appointed in September in line with regulations governing publicly listed companies.

The deal has now entered its final administrative phase, which involves the admission to trading of the newly issued shares before the Spanish National Securities Market Commission (CNMV). According to the company, this process will not affect either the effectiveness of the transaction or the day-to-day operations of Bodegas Riojanas.

Expanding Vintae's portfolio

The acquisition adds several wineries and assets to the Vintae portfolio, including Bodegas Riojanas in Rioja, Bodegas Torreduero in Toro, Bodegas Viore in Rueda and a 56% stake in Veiga Naúm in Rías Baixas, together with their production assets, vineyards, facilities and commercial brands.

Founded in 1890, Bodegas Riojanas owns brands including Monte Real and Viña Albina. Vintae said the transaction marks the beginning of a new chapter for the winery, supported by a strengthened financial structure and the backing of a group with a commercial presence in Spain and more than 70 countries worldwide.

The company added that it will define Bodegas Riojanas' medium- and long-term development plan while reviewing the positioning of the group's companies and brands and gradually integrating processes, systems and management models.

Richi Arambarri, CEO of Vintae, said: "This transaction represents another important step in Vintae's evolution. Over the past few years, we have built a company with projects that are deeply rooted in their territories, supported by a solid business structure and strong commercial capabilities. That journey is what now enables us to take on a project of this scale with confidence and responsibility."

He added: "We begin this new chapter with the utmost respect for the identity of Bodegas Riojanas and with the conviction that we can help drive its future while preserving the value of its history and its brands."
